channel.title: The Joy of x
channel.description: The acclaimed mathematician and author Steven Strogatz interviews some of the world's leading scientists about their lives and work.
channel.itunes:summary:
The acclaimed mathematician and author Steven Strogatz interviews some of the world's leading scientists about their lives and work.
You've learned from Quanta. Now we want to learn from you! Quanta is conducting a series of surveys to better serve our audience. Take our podcast listener survey and you will be entered to win a free Quanta book, t-shirt or tote bag: quantamag.typeform.com/podcast
channel.itunes:author: Steven Strogatz and Quanta Magazine
channel.itunes:subtitle: The Joy of x

item.title: Eve Marder on the Crucial Resilience of Neurons
item.description:
Eve Marder’s research into the plasticity and resilience of nervous systems finds universal principles guiding life’s responses to stress.

The post Eve Marder on the Crucial Resilience of Neurons first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Eve Marder on the Crucial Resilience of Neurons
item.itunes:summary: Behaviors are sometimes described as being "hardwired," but the work of the celebrated neuroscientist Eve Marder of Brandeis University has explored a crucial difference between neural circuits and engineered ones: Neurons need to be resilient in the face of their own ongoing biomolecular transformation. In this episode, host Steven Strogatz talks with Marder about "multiple solutions" as a key feature of life, the similarities between a crab's stomach and our shoulders, and the secret to a satisfying career in science. Read more at https://www.quantamagazine.org/eve-marder-on-the-crucial-resilience-of-neurons-20210517/.
item.itunes:author: Steven Strogatz and Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:subtitle: Behaviors are sometimes described as being "hardwired," but the work of the celebrated neuroscientist Eve Marder of Brandeis University has explored a crucial difference between neural circuits and engineered ones: Neurons need to be resilient in the face of their own ongoing biomolecular transformation. In this episode, host Steven Strogatz talks with Marder about "multiple solutions" as a key feature of life, the similarities between a crab's stomach and our shoulders, and the secret to a satisfying career in science. Read more at https://www.quantamagazine.org/eve-marder-on-the-crucial-resilience-of-neurons-20210517/.
item.content:encoded:
Eve Marder’s research into the plasticity and resilience of nervous systems finds universal principles guiding life’s responses to stress.

The post Eve Marder on the Crucial Resilience of Neurons first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Charlie Marcus Knows That Quantum Facts Aren’t Complicated
item.description:
The secret to making a qubit for future quantum computers might depend on knowing how to tie knots in unusual materials, argues the physicist Charlie Marcus.

The post Charlie Marcus Knows That Quantum Facts Aren’t Complicated first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Charlie Marcus Knows That Quantum Facts Aren’t Complicated
item.content:encoded:
The secret to making a qubit for future quantum computers might depend on knowing how to tie knots in unusual materials, argues the physicist Charlie Marcus.

The post Charlie Marcus Knows That Quantum Facts Aren’t Complicated first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Amie Wilkinson Sees the Dynamic Chaos in Puff Pastry
item.description:
To a dynamicist like Amie Wilkinson, understanding the universe is about knowing all the right moves.

The post Amie Wilkinson Sees the Dynamic Chaos in Puff Pastry first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Amie Wilkinson Sees the Dynamic Chaos in Puff Pastry
item.content:encoded:
To a dynamicist like Amie Wilkinson, understanding the universe is about knowing all the right moves.

The post Amie Wilkinson Sees the Dynamic Chaos in Puff Pastry first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Emery Brown and the Truth About Anesthesia
item.description:
Anesthesia is very different from sleep — which is why it offers unique opportunities for studying the human brain, says the physician-researcher and statistician Emery Brown.

The post Emery Brown and the Truth About Anesthesia first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Emery Brown and the Truth About Anesthesia
item.content:encoded:
Anesthesia is very different from sleep — which is why it offers unique opportunities for studying the human brain, says the physician-researcher and statistician Emery Brown.

The post Emery Brown and the Truth About Anesthesia first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Melanie Mitchell Takes AI Research Back to Its Roots
item.description:
To build a general artificial intelligence, we may need to know more about our own minds, argues the computer scientist Melanie Mitchell.

The post Melanie Mitchell Takes AI Research Back to Its Roots first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Melanie Mitchell Takes AI Research Back to Its Roots
item.content:encoded:
To build a general artificial intelligence, we may need to know more about our own minds, argues the computer scientist Melanie Mitchell.

The post Melanie Mitchell Takes AI Research Back to Its Roots first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Trachette Jackson Fights Cancer With Math
item.description:
Quantitative models built by the mathematical biologist Trachette Jackson can make cancer therapies safer and more effective.

The post Trachette Jackson Fights Cancer With Math first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Trachette Jackson Fights Cancer With Math
item.content:encoded:
Quantitative models built by the mathematical biologist Trachette Jackson can make cancer therapies safer and more effective.

The post Trachette Jackson Fights Cancer With Math first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Rediet Abebe on Using Algorithms for Social Justice
item.description:
The computer scientist Rediet Abebe’s passion for applied mathematics closely aligns with her passion to solve problems with poverty and social inequality.

The post Rediet Abebe on Using Algorithms for Social Justice first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Rediet Abebe on Using Algorithms for Social Justice
item.content:encoded:
The computer scientist Rediet Abebe’s passion for applied mathematics closely aligns with her passion to solve problems with poverty and social inequality.

The post Rediet Abebe on Using Algorithms for Social Justice first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Federico Ardila on Math, Music and the Space of Possibilities
item.description:
The mathematician Federico Ardila takes a creative approach to the search for useful answers hiding among inconceivably huge numbers of possible ones.

The post Federico Ardila on Math, Music and the Space of Possibilities first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Federico Ardila on Math, Music and the Space of Possibilities
item.itunes:summary: Federico Ardila, born in Colombia and now a professor mathematics at San Francisco State University, is an expert in the field of combinatorics, the study of all the possible configurations of finite systems. This week, Ardila talks with host Steven Strogatz about the importance of feelings in mathematics, the music of collaboration, imagining higher dimensional spaces, and the art and science of exploring "the space of possibilities." This episode was produced by Dana Bialek. Read more at https://www.quantamagazine.org/federico-ardila-on-math-music-and-the-space-of-possibilities-20210329/. Production and original music by Story Mechanics.
item.itunes:author: Steven Strogatz and Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:subtitle: Federico Ardila, born in Colombia and now a professor mathematics at San Francisco State University, is an expert in the field of combinatorics, the study of all the possible configurations of finite systems. This week, Ardila talks with host Steven Strogatz about the importance of feelings in mathematics, the music of collaboration, imagining higher dimensional spaces, and the art and science of exploring "the space of possibilities." This episode was produced by Dana Bialek. Read more at https://www.quantamagazine.org/federico-ardila-on-math-music-and-the-space-of-possibilities-20210329/. Production and original music by Story Mechanics.
item.content:encoded:
The mathematician Federico Ardila takes a creative approach to the search for useful answers hiding among inconceivably huge numbers of possible ones.

The post Federico Ardila on Math, Music and the Space of Possibilities first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Sharon Glotzer’s Deep Curiosity About Order From Chaos
item.description:
The computational physicist Sharon Glotzer finds unifying principles that structure the chaotic dance of the particles that make up matter.

The post Sharon Glotzer’s Deep Curiosity About Order From Chaos first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Sharon Glotzer’s Deep Curiosity About Order From Chaos
item.content:encoded:
The computational physicist Sharon Glotzer finds unifying principles that structure the chaotic dance of the particles that make up matter.

The post Sharon Glotzer’s Deep Curiosity About Order From Chaos first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Frank Wilczek on the Strong Force, Quarks and Dark Matter
item.description:
The theoretical physicist Frank Wilczek explained what holds atomic nuclei together, and he is still pushing at the limits of what the standard model can tell us.

The post Frank Wilczek on the Strong Force, Quarks and Dark Matter first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Frank Wilczek on the Strong Force, Quarks and Dark Matter
item.content:encoded:
The theoretical physicist Frank Wilczek explained what holds atomic nuclei together, and he is still pushing at the limits of what the standard model can tell us.

The post Frank Wilczek on the Strong Force, Quarks and Dark Matter first appeared on Quanta Magazine

item.title: Bonnie Bassler on Talkative Bacteria and Eavesdropping Viruses
item.description:
The molecular biologist Bonnie Bassler is deciphering the chemical languages that bacteria use to coordinate their assaults on a host.

The post Bonnie Bassler on Talkative Bacteria and Eavesdropping Viruses first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Bonnie Bassler on Talkative Bacteria and Eavesdropping Viruses

item.title: Neil Shubin on Tiktaalik, Ballistic Tongues and Evolution
item.description:
The paleontologist Neil Shubin talks with host Steven Strogatz about hunting for a 375 million-year-old fossil and finding novel traits that evolved many times.

The post Neil Shubin on Tiktaalik, Ballistic Tongues and Evolution first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Neil Shubin on Tiktaalik, Ballistic Tongues and Evolution

item.title: Podcast Preview: The Joy of x, Season Two
item.description:
Hosted by Steven Strogatz, The Joy of x podcast is back with new episodes that explore the inner worlds of top-tier scientists and mathematicians like Frank Wilczek, Bonnie Bassler and Amie Wilkinson.

The post Podcast Preview: The Joy of x, Season Two first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Podcast Preview: The Joy of x, Season Two

item.title: Moon Duchin on Fair Voting and Random Walks
item.description:
Can geometry save democracy from gerrymandering? Mathematician Moon Duchin discusses the possibilities with host Steven Strogatz.

The post Moon Duchin on Fair Voting and Random Walks first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Moon Duchin on Fair Voting and Random Walks

item.title: Brian Keating’s Quest for the Origin of the Universe
item.description:
The astrophysicist Brian Keating talks to host Steven Strogatz about chasing the universe’s greatest mysteries — and what it’s like to have a major discovery slip through his fingers.

The post Brian Keating’s Quest for the Origin of the Universe first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Brian Keating’s Quest for the Origin of the Universe

item.title: Rebecca Goldin and Brian Nosek on Hard Truths in Math and Psychology
item.description:
The mathematician Rebecca Goldin and the psychology researcher Brian Nosek speak with host Steven Strogatz about what it’s like to be the bearers of unpopular truths.

The post Rebecca Goldin and Brian Nosek on Hard Truths in Math and Psychology first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Rebecca Goldin and Brian Nosek on Hard Truths in Math and Psychology

item.title: Cori Bargmann on the Genetics of Transparent Worms, Supertasters and Cancer
item.description:
The neurogenetics pioneer Cori Bargmann speaks with host Steven Strogatz about why a transparent worm became her favorite animal and how a genetic discovery she made inspired a revolutionary cancer treatment.

The post Cori Bargmann on the Genetics of Transparent Worms, Supertasters and Cancer first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Cori Bargmann on the Genetics of Transparent Worms, Supertasters and Cancer

item.title: Tadashi Tokieda's Special Kind of Magic
item.description:
The mathematician Tadashi Tokieda and host Steven Strogatz explore what we can learn about the world from simple “toys” with remarkable physical or mathematical properties.

The post Tadashi Tokieda’s Special Kind of Magic first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Tadashi Tokieda's Special Kind of Magic
item.itunes:summary: The mathematician Tadashi Tokieda loves to explore the special mathematical and physical properties of the simple objects that he calls "toys" - and he's passionate about sharing what they can teach us about the world. In this episode, he takes host Steven Strogatz on a conversational tour of some of his toys' surprises and talks about his life as an artist and classical philologist before he became a professor of mathematics at Stanford University. This episode was produced by Dana Bialek. Read more at QuantaMagazine.org. Production and original music by Story Mechanics.
item.itunes:author: Steven Strogatz and Quanta Magazine

item.title: Janna Levin on Seeing and Hearing Black Holes
item.description:
The astrophysicist Janna Levin describes the fierce scientific beauty she finds in black holes and reveals why she took a major risk early in her career.

The post Janna Levin on Seeing and Hearing Black Holes first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Janna Levin on Seeing and Hearing Black Holes

item.title: John Urschel: From NFL Player to Mathematician
item.description:
John Urschel, who retired from playing professional football with the Baltimore Ravens to become a mathematician, talks to host Steven Strogatz about the fascinations of graph theory that lured him away from the NFL.

The post John Urschel: From NFL Player to Mathematician first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: John Urschel: From NFL Player to Mathematician

item.title: Corina Tarnita and the Deep Mathematics of Social Insects
item.description:
The mathematical biologist Corina Tarnita explains to host Steven Strogatz how quantitative modeling solved the mystery of fairy circles.

The post Corina Tarnita and the Deep Mathematics of Social Insects first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Corina Tarnita and the Deep Mathematics of Social Insects

item.title: Robbert Dijkgraaf on Exploring Quantum Reality
item.description:
The mathematical physicist Robbert Dijkgraaf and host Steven Strogatz discuss the frontiers of string theory and why space and time might not be the most fundamental things in the universe.

The post Robbert Dijkgraaf on Exploring Quantum Reality first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Robbert Dijkgraaf on Exploring Quantum Reality

item.title: Leslie Vosshall on Designer Mosquitoes and Dude Walls
item.description:
Events take an interesting turn after the neurogeneticist Leslie Vosshall speaks with host Steven Strogatz about ways to make mosquitoes less deadly and the obstacles facing educational inclusiveness.

The post Leslie Vosshall on Designer Mosquitoes and Dude Walls first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Leslie Vosshall on Designer Mosquitoes and Dude Walls

item.title: Alex Kontorovich on the Absolute Truth of Pure Math
item.description:
The mathematician Alex Kontorovich speaks with host Steven Strogatz about regaining his creative freedom in an intimidating collaboration and about the pleasures of spherical geometry.

The post Alex Kontorovich on the Absolute Truth of Pure Math first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Alex Kontorovich on the Absolute Truth of Pure Math

item.title: Priya Natarajan on Black Holes and Mapping the Universe
item.description:
In this episode, Priya Natarajan, professor of astronomy and physics at Yale University, speaks with Steven Strogatz about her lifelong fascinations, including black holes, mapping the universe and early personal computers.

The post Priya Natarajan on Black Holes and Mapping the Universe first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Priya Natarajan on Black Holes and Mapping the Universe

item.title: Podcast Preview: Introducing The Joy of x
item.description:
Hosted by Steven Strogatz, The Joy of x podcast opens a window into the inner worlds of top-tier scientists and mathematicians while shining light on universal themes like creativity, collaboration or navigating professional challenges.

The post Podcast Preview: Introducing The Joy of x first appeared on Quanta Magazine
item.itunes:title: Podcast Preview: Introducing The Joy of x
